channel.title: Character Systems on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Character Systems on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Temporary Buffs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about temporary buffs.

And I mean. What I mean by that are the buffs you get for a short period of time for having a spell cast on you or drinking a potion or maybe eating food in an RPG. This is based on a large set of questions asked by future Astrominer. I’m going to read all their questions, then I’m going to talk about it a little bit and then I’m going to go through each question in order and answer it. This is going to be long and it’s going to reference a lot of videos, but that’s actually something I need to talk about after I read you the questions. So let’s start.

item.title: Arcanum Fate Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum Fate Points.

Yes, I want to talk about a very particular feature in a specific game that’s almost 25 years old. But this comes from a question from Beefy NFN. I know what that means. Wait, no, I don’t. I have no idea.

Let’s continue. Asked: “I’d really like to hear you go into detail about how you balanced Arcanum with fate points. As someone who has never seen gameplay of Arcanum, let alone played it, The idea of fate points is kind of offputting to me as a game mechanic. As a player, I tend to hoard items that are one use and only use them when absolutely necessary, which means I end the game with a bunch of set items. So I’m curious how that applies in Arcanum with Fate Points.

item.title: Using Combat Skills Outside Of Combat
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using combat skills outside of combat.

This video can be seen as a companion piece to yesterday’s video about using non-combat skills in combat. And I’m filming this the day after the previous video, but the previous video is probably one you watched two days ago. I’ll link it below And I also want to remind you guys, it’s still June for me, so I haven’t seen your comments on that video. I thought I’d make this video before I saw your comments, so we can kind of compare our ideas on, in this case, usage making, utility for combat skills outside of combat. So you may want to watch the other one first. In any case, I’ll also start off by saying this has been done before. I know Fallout did it, I know The Outer Worlds did it.

item.title: Using Non Combat Skills In Combat
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using non-combat skills in combat.

This comes from a question from IE50 — IE50 - IE50, who asked: “I don’t know if you’ve done a video on this, but I was wondering about your thoughts on how traditionally non-combat skills can assist in combat. You’ve mentioned how skill-based games can result in players making dud characters because they end up sucking in combat. What about having those skills have related perks or actions that are valuable in combat? Or the more common option is having certain combat actions scale off. Non-combat skills Examples: intelligent scale skills, scale medicine, charisma, skills for team buffs, etc.

item.title: Respec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Funky Town, directions 2 and subloations within.

But oh no, today I’m talking about respec because I’m answering a question from protect one cloud, who asked: “Do you have a video on player respec, resetting talents, stats, character, investments? If not, might make a good video to go over”. Protect one cloud, You’re right, I haven’t made a video on this. I’ve mentioned it here and there in different videos, usually as an example of a feature that some people love, some people hate, or is there for balance reasons or meeting a design goal or whatever. But let me let’s talk about respec specifically and that way I can talk about all that in one spot and then point people to the one video. I think before I start, I should probably define respspec, because it means slightly different things to a lot of people. I’m talking about respec. My definition for respec is when a game lets you get back all of your skill perk attribute spell. Points, any combination of those, to respend them. Uh, some games let you change class too. I think that is a little rare and a little more extreme For purposes of this video. Think of respec as you’re allowed to rele the character that you’ve made, as if they were gaining all those levels again for the first time. Now, historically, respect didn’t used to exist.

item.title: Attributes Limiting Skills
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about attributes, limiting skills or, more specifically, what a.

A commenter asked a question about contradictions between attributes and skills. So let me let me give the question first and then I’ll dive into my response. The question was from Deja Vu6196, who asked: “Hi Tim, what are your thoughts on contradictions between attributes and skills? How can a player with a one intelligence reach 100 in the science skill? Should they be allowed to, or should the attribute cap the max skill they can reach”. Taking a specific example from Fallout New Vegas, the same one character who has a dumb dialogue can somehow reach speech 100 and logically talk legate Lanias into retreating, which also seems contradictory.

item.title: The Premade Character
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pre-made character.

This comes from a question from Wes 9353, who asked: “Hey, Uncle Tim, do you have a video on the preset character? What determines the stats of the preset character and who determines the preset character”. I call these pre-made characters. Um, it’s just the term I use. I tried to do this with saying preset and I kept saying pre-made. So I finally decided I’m just going to call this the pre-made character and say why I’m not calling it preset. I don’t do this a lot, if you’ve played a lot of my RPGs, you’ll find that most of them do not have pre-made characters. I encourage people to make their own.

item.title: Skill Trees
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about skill trees.

This is a question from every Nova, not any Nova. Every Nova who asked: “Can you please record a video about RPG skill trees? I’m interested specifically in the skill tree as a model for acquiring skills. Different types of skill trees, different point allocation models, even different shapes of skill trees as a different way to force the player into specific choices” sometimes.

item.title: "Dead Levels"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about cheese.

I really like cheese. Cheese is good on everything. I really want some cheese.

No wait, today we’re talking about dead levels. I want to answer a question from commenter Ender. I’m hoping answer. I’m pronouncing that right.

Ender asked: what are your thoughts on dead levels or level UPS or the equivalent, where characters get nothing at all or only the standard stuff for Levering, such as hit points, magic points, skill points, but no new spells, perks or notable character abilities? When are these dead levels wise to include, if at all, and how do they affect players, designers and World building? He goes on to say: when I said this was too complex of an this, I said this was too complex of an answer for me to leave a comment for. So I said I’d do a video. And he said: well, just to give you an example from Temple of Elemental Evil, meaning D and D 3.5, a fighter at level five is a dead level, as our most odd fighter levels. Sorcerer 2 is also mostly a dead level, since it doesn’t Grant any new spells or spell levels, class features. It’s a good question and it was very cheeky using one of my own games as an example, so I liked it, and also this question has a long enough, nuanced enough. I know I use that word a lot, but I’m basically saying I don’t like truly dead levels, but I have to explain, like where my cutof point is for me considering a level dead and of course, yeah, you can see games of mine that have dead levels. I’ll point out that Temple — and I think I’ve mentioned this before — it’s one of those games where I didn’t create the setting, the story or the system mechanics. So I did get a couple of my own ideas in there, but for the most, part that dead level was straight out of 3.5. And don’t misunderstand me, 3.5 is my favorite DN d game. I mentioned it on my D and video.

item.title: Action Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about action points.

This is an interesting question. It comes from tact DB who asks: where do you draw the line and how granular the action point should be for one’s game — AP resolution, if you will. Some RPGs use a base 100 AP for every character with equipment and status as MiFi modifiers. Everything you do, from opening your backpack to raising your weapon costs AP. Some actions even activate an AP drain, like moving with your Shield up costs more than just having it default.

item.title: Player Power From Build vs. Items
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about player power versus from builds versus from items.

This is a question from Anubis 1101 who, as a super mutant, got a priority video. So I stopped what I was doing, which was organizing my collection of amusing socks and underwear, to talk about this. So let’s dive into it. Anubis1 asked — it’s very long, Tim.

Are you familiar with the myth of the gun versus the myth of the sword? It’s an ideological approach to fiction storytelling that I really love because it meshes well with game design. In essence, it describes where the character or player’s power comes from. The myth of the sword is when the character’s power comes from their own skill or stats. It emphasizes that power comes from within. Most fantasy settings lean heavily on this. The myth of the gun places the source of skills and abilities in the equipment, emphasizing finding and creating better gear.

item.title: Why I Wanted Luck In Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I wanted luck in Fallout.

It’s for me early February, I’ve been snowed in for a few days and this is a fun Friday video and I figured why not? If you remember, I talked about when Fallout had to be converted from gerps to special. Chris Taylor had a home brew system he wanted to use, which we later added perks to when Brian Fargo said: hey, I want something for people to buy, besid skills, but the attributes were just strength, perception, endurance, Charisma, intelligence and Agility. There was no luck I I talk about this in the how we made Fallout special vid. I really, really wanted luck to be in there because I had had a ton of fun with luck based characters in gerps, especially GP supers, and in Champions and in a few other tabletop role playing games that had luck in them. Um, if you watch my interplay after hours video, I talk about a character I had called lucky boy who bought all the different luck, uh, advantages or powers that were in the tabletop I was using and was just insanely fun to play. It wasn’t that he was good at anything, it’s just he frequently got lucky and scored critical hits or Critical with the skill and then anyone who tried to attack him frequently messed up and hurt themselves and other people around them. It was fun, because of that. I wanted in Fallout, I wanted and something like that. First of all, I wanted it as an attribute, because I remember when I brought it up people were like, well, we’re just throw in perk, a luck perk and I’m like, no, as a perk that means some people won’t have it. So when you go, what’s the luck on them?

item.title: Character Build Fairness
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about character build fairness.

Now let me give you some context. I got a question from a viewer, JP valheim, who asked about fairness in video games and I replied that I wasn’t sure what that meant in the context of mostly what I’ve made, which are single player RPGs. And I said, outside of accessibility features and making sure that any character build that the player makes can finish the game — that I didn’t really have an answer and he replied, and I quote: you mentioned single player games and the fairness of any build being able to complete the game. Could you elaborate on why exactly it is so bad if a certain build cannot complete the game? I would argue that it’s not the build not being able to complete the game. That’s the problem.

item.title: Arcanum Backgrounds
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about Arcanum backgrounds and I will talk a little bit about them.

I want to give you some context, but then I will answer this question from Feliciano frontado, 3134, who asked: fun Friday suggestion: what were your favorite backgrounds in Arcanum? This is fun to go look at because I loved backgrounds in Arcanum and in fact I kind of want give you a little background on backgrounds. See what I did there. It wasn’t funny.

item.title: Status Effect Implementation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about status effect implementation.

Why am I talking about this? Somebody recently said, hey, I really liked your quest implementation video and I’m like I haven’t really done like a kind of a more nitty-gritty implementation video in a while. So let me talk about this.

Status effects are what I call any effect that’s placed on an object in the world. It’s usually a creature, like you know, you may throw a DOT on a creature, so every now then they have fire on them and every now and then they take fire damage, but not always. You might throw a spell on a door that says this door is being held, you know, like in place, like a, like a hold portal spell from DND. Or you may summon a weapon, or you may summon a zombie, and all those are what I call status effects, because they place an effect in the world that usually has a particular running time and then they cleans it up.

item.title: Deckbuilder RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about deck builder RPGs.

Those are role playing games where your abilities, or sometimes even more than that, are given to you randomly from a shuffle deck. This is not my area of expertise, but wami Bonito 2558 asked: would you have any opinions on carddeck Builder based art pgs, where character abilities are represented by cards and are also not guaranteed to play out the same way every combat, since in some fights you might never draw a particular card? Yes, I will tell you something. My response — you guessed it — is nuanced. I originally wrote a short response to this comment, but it wasn’t expressing the way I felt and I Al also thought that in writing it looked negative, which I’ve been told. Sometimes emails, my Memoir, comes across as negative and then when I say it, people are like: oh, I like it’s not negative at all. So I figured I’d do that with my answer to this. So, deck builder RPGs. Let me tell you why I like them. I haven’t played that many, but I will tell you why I like them and also why I like the — the whole idea behind that mechanic.

item.title: Wildstar Class Summary (Nov 2008)
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to do the Wild Star class summary from November 2008.

I found this file from 2008 that I created at home because I’ve mentioned in my other wstar videos that I, when I became design director, I was given 90 days to completely design all the classes for the game, because there was a big meeting in November. And I actually have It’s November 13th 2018, so that was about 90 days after I started as design director with all except for one person — all new design hires.

item.title: Solo vs. Party
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Solo versus party character control.

This is something that was asked most recently. I think it’s been asked several times, but it asked most recently by Tom FS, who asked: I was wondering if you could go over your thoughts on full party control versus only controlling your character. I view this separately from the full party creation versus individual character creation debate. What’s the lack of party control in Arcanum due to multiplayer? I know it was that case for never winner nights.

item.title: Perk Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about perk ideas.

This is a fun Friday video, so it’s kind of a me talking about ideas I’ve had in my idea book for years and I pulled out ones that were specific to perks. I wanted to do — and I’m going to talk about — reactions. I got to these perk ideas when I talked about them in the past and also why you don’t see these in any of my games. Get to all that, but first let’s just dive into some of the ideas I had for new perks. One of the ones I’ve always been noodling around is using your charisma attribute to replace your deck attribute for Armor class bonuses. Basically, whatever dexterity is providing to make you harder to hit, you can take a perk and say that bonus comes from my Charisma instead of my dexterity.

item.title: Encumbrance, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about encumbrance again.

So this is encumbrance part two. The video — uh, the part one video, just called incumbrance, and I’ll link it below — generated a lot of really good discussion in the comment section. So if you haven’t watched it, you probably want to go watch that before you watch this, because I’m going to build on some things from the first part and I’m not really going to dive into details. So if you haven’t watched encumbrance, just stop this, go watch that and then come back here. The first thing I noticed was a lot of people were saying how I was talking about encumbrance as weight based. So items had weight, you could only carry a maximum weight and, um, there were things like perks and attributes that could increase that weight and they suggested doing it with slots and there’s multiple ways of doing it I mean, our Arcanum had a grid and you had to fit everything on that grid and sometimes you had to Tetris things around to get a new object, and there are other games that did you have like everything. Every item just fits into one slot, and you start with 20 slots, and maybe there are bags you can find that increase your slots. Maybe there are perks that say every bag you pick up has two extra slots, whatever it is the My point stands, though, that encumbrance is about imposing an inventory limit about how much the player can carry. So whether you do it with weight or whether you do it with slot, whether it’s multiple slots, whether it’s slot and weight, those are just limits. So we’re still talking about the same thing. It’s just: how is your encumbrance set up to limit the number of items that a player could carry at any given point? Then several of you said, well, just don’t have.

item.title: Elemental Mage
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. It’s Friday funay, so today I wanted to talk about an idea I had for a class called the elemental Mage.

Now this comes from my idea book, which is always near me, although it’s actually in one of the older books behind me. I had this idea a long time ago. I wrote it down in detail after I left carbine because I had designed a class for wild star called the Elementalist. That had been scrapped, but I still noodled about that idea and I just wanted to kind of get the class concept written down.

item.title: Conflating Player Skill With Character Skill
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about conflating player skill with player character skill.

Now I’m answering a question by Vol forac, who asks how to resolve the conflict between player skill and their character’s ability in an action RPG. To elaborate with an example: Fallout in the past was all about skill checks and dice rolls, but starting with Fallout 3, the first person shooter aspect became more emphasized with each release. Where’s the line between letting the player be a good shot because the game mechanics allow them and the player being a good shot because they built a character? That is a good shot. Um, first of all, that’s a good question.

item.title: The Outer Worlds: Eight Playthroughs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about eight playthroughs of The Outer Worlds.

Now I do have a video where I talk about play testing The Outer Worlds and I say I did it 16 times and I give a very high level overview of the playthroughs I did. I was digging through archives and I found much more detail on the first eight playthroughs. I did so, if this seems familiar, I’ll link the other video below, which is a very high level, but I’m going to actually read what I wrote for that also when I’m done. I found a list of purchase requests. It was a document I kept.

item.title: Skill-based XP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about skill-based XP.

I did a video a few weeks ago where I talked about what I thought the best way to give XP was, which spoiler warning is: through quests, because that’s the way I thought most encouraged players of any build to advance through your game. This generated a ton of discussion — really good discussion, by the way. It was a perfect example of why I like doing this channel. I was reading all these comments, I responded to some of them and it made me think, so I thought, hey, let’s do a video on skill-based XP. Um, I now I’ve done a video on class based versus skill-based systems that you also may want to watch. It’s these videos will be linked below. Um, you just may want to watch it first to know what I’m talking about, but I’m at a high level.

item.title: Companions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about companions.

Got a lot of questions about Companions and how I feel like they should be used in RPGs and I don’t have a simple answer, which is why I put off answering this for such a long time, and I’ve gotten so many qu different questions from different people that I will try to cover them all in this one video. So it’s. It covers what — whether or not I think companions should be in games, and how many there should be and are they controllable, and what’s their involvement in the quest line, and blah. So let’s just dive in. There’s a lot of topics here. Companions are a nuanced topic, so, companions, let’s start with whether or not they’re controllable. In general, in isometric games, I lean towards them being controllable — that once they enter the party, yes, you control them.

item.title: Skill Design in RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about skill design in RPGs in particular.

Queen of bad jokes 9413 asks: I wondered how you would approach skills for a new game. How do you choose? What skill types are there? How do you and would you level them? Is there a particular skill that has worked out for multiple games? How can you design for balancing? And are there skill tables too? I mean testing besides in the testing rooms and player testing in the alpha Queen.

item.title: The Evolution Of Flaws
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the evolution of flaws.

If you played The Outer Worlds, you know in there flaws is a system mechanic where as you play the game, it pays attention to things you do and then suggest, suggests a flaw you know, like maybe you’re scared of robots, or maybe you take a lot of falling damage — a lot, or you’re, maybe you’re afraid of heights because you take a lot of falling damage. The idea for that germinated almost 37 years before that game. Let me walk you through this, and I think this is a good parallel to the video I did on why ideas aren’t worthwhile — because this idea, I mean, came to me when I was a teenager but it took decades for it to be honed into something that I put into an RPG the way I wanted it to be. So let’s talk about that, so I know I’ve told these stories. I’m going to kind of repeat them here a little bit. Just for completeness, but back in high school when I was playing D and D, there was a couple times where low character stats led to really interesting characters. One of them was dumb dialogue which actually ended up in Fallout.

item.title: Level Scaling
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about level scaling.

Had a lot of questions about level scaling, especially after I did some of the videos on Random Encounters and how I did it in both Fallout and Arcanum, so I thought I’d talk about level scaling and how my ideas on it changed over time. But let me go ahead and uh give one of the questions that many, many people asked. This one is from Angel vodka 2984, who asks: what do you think about level scaling? Do you like the game world and enemies to scale up to your characters level so you get more or less constant challenge, or do you prefer a fixed level system in which some areas get easier over time but you get a more real sense of progression?

item.title: Class-based vs. Skill-based RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about class versus skill based role-playing games.

I’ve read a lot of discussion online about this for years. Some people think classes are the way to go, their original, their how things were done a long time ago. They’re a tried and true and tested. I’ve heard other people say, no, skills are the way to go, they’re the best, they’re the new wave, even though they’ve been around for 20 years. I think that the real answer, of course, is: uh, a little one, little the other. So what I wanted to do is talk about the pros and cons of class-based systems, because I realized every Pro of class is a con of skill and vice versa. So I’ll talk about.

item.title: Arcanum Tech Skills & Schematics
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum Tech skills and schematics, and the reason I want to talk about that is

I’ve had enough videos where I’ve talked about designs, changing other people having better design ideas than me, the whole process of doing a game from start to finish with pillars, design pillars and so many other considerations that the reason I want to talk about Arcanum Tech skills is. I want to talk about something you probably haven’t heard about, which is they started completely differently than how they ended up. I was going through my Troika file archive, which is really big, and I found the original master design dock for Arcanum. That was written in early 90s, late 98, early 99, and I found that originally there were a lot more Tech skills than what we planned. So let me walk you through. What happened originally.

item.title: Picking Your Best Class in AD&D
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about picking your best class in a, d and d.

Why? Because I found This is something I sent to Dragon publishing. Oh, it’s not gonna work. Um in 1980, Something the postmark has been worn off for a submission to be published in the dragon magazine, although I think they dropped the by then used to be the dragon and then it just became dragon. But I submitted this to Roger Moore who was the editor, saying: hey, you wrote something like this, but since then Unearthed arcan has come out.

item.title: Attribute Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about attributes in role-playing games in general, because the last couple days

I’ve talked about specifically the attributes in Fallout and in Arcanum and made me think, wow, you know, not only have my thoughts changed since then there. I’ve been in so many discussions about attributes, I’ve seen lots of RPGs that handle our attributes differently. Plus, someone even mentioned in the comments that I was saying how Dex was so overpowered in gerps in a much earlier video where I talked about the gerps apps I made, including a combat one, a combat simulator. But then in Fallout and in Arcanum I did the same thing. And you’re right, I’m totally guilty. I’m.

item.title: Arcanum Attribute Effects
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to go over the effects of the attributes in Arcanum.

Yesterday I talked about the effects of the special attributes in Fallout. People really seem to like that, so I did. I’m going to go through our Canon today. Let me address: yes, my shirt is super wrinkly. I got this at the very bottom of the box I found in the garage. It is when we shipped Arcanum and yet this is an Arcanum shirt.

item.title: Fallout SPECIAL Attribute Effects
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the attributes in Fallout, the special attributes.

Now I’m going to talk about exactly what they do. Part of the reason I want to do this is that almost a year ago, someone on Reddit was saying how they like games that have luck in them, and I explained what luck did in Fallout like in detail and I realized I had a document that about the time we shipped her shortly after, I wrote down exactly what all the attributes did, and I still have that document, um. But I know I had originally written it down on paper, so I went to go look for it. And what I was looking for, by the way, I found this: is a original Fallout shirt that still says gurps on it.

item.title: Arcanum's Original Attribute System
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Arcanum attribute system and specifically the original attributes, the way they were designed, not the ones we shipped with, and I’m answering a question.

Someone asked — I’m sorry about this. Like over a month ago, maybe almost six weeks ago, somebody asked what the how I came up with the beauty stat for Arcanum which made me look at the design dock, which made me look at the original design dock, which made me go whoa, I was planning to do something entirely different, so let me talk about that. So, brief refresher: if you remember the attributes for Arcanum, there were eight of them. They were physical and mental, and then there were these four categories called Power, resistant, resistance, prowess, which I’ve never liked an appearance. So the physical ones were strength, con, Dex and Beauty, and the mental ones were intelligence, will, power, perception and charisma.

item.title: The Arcanum One-Point Fiasco
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what I now call, in very distant hindsight, the Arcanum.

One point, Fiasco, but let me give you context. I love to give context, so recently I had someone who was a director — director - director at a company — ask me how I made decisions as a director. Um, they were often asked to make decisions on things that they weren’t quite sure about. Either it wasn’t in their area of expertise or they just weren’t sure. There were multiple Solutions and they weren’t sure. And at the end of this video I’ll tell you what I told them, not because I don’t want to tell you now, but because you need to understand big things that happened to me to understand why I gave this person the answer I did now. I talked about in The Fallout video that there were decisions made that didn’t 100 agree with.

item.title: Arcanum 2 System Extensions
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to talk about Arcanum 2..

Specifically the system mechanics extensions that I designed for it, because I was reading through them recently and I’m like, hey, these are pretty cool. I kind of wish we had done this. Just want to point out before I begin I do not own the rights to Arcanum. Those are owned by Activision Blizzard, so there’s no need to ask if I’m working on an Arcanum 2 or can make an Arcanum to. I can’t ask Activision Blizzard if you have any questions. So I was going through my design folder from Troika and I found one that was literally called Arcanum 2 system extensions.

item.title: How Fallout was made SPECIAL
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how, in just two weeks, right near the end, about four or five months before Fallout shipped, we replaced gurps with special.

Now this isn’t going to be a video about why we had to do that — that’s another video, maybe, if I ever feel like talking about that. But what I want to talk about is: have we managed to replace such an integral part of what you would think would be a game so quickly? And there’s really two reasons for that that I’ll get into. But they were that Chris Taylor, our lead designer, already had several Homebrew systems and they had one that looked really good and I’ll tell you how, what that was and how we integrated it. And then the other reason we could replace, uh, the system mechanics so quickly was the nature of how Fallout came to be, and I know — I’ve talked and you may have read other places, that Fallout started very small, uh, just me, and then I was really championing Gertz. So let me talk about both of those.

item.title: Why I Like Flawed Characters
item.description:
Hi, it’s me Tim.

I’ve gotten some great feedback on the dumb dialogue video where I talk about the origin of dumb dialogue being restrictions that I put on players in my original d campaign back in late 70s, early 80s. So I wanted to talk about why I like flawed player characters so much and why I think restrictions lead to better role-playing and just better game playing in general. So, after playing d in high school, I really got into groups in grad school and that’s where I first discovered disadvantages, which is in gerp’s, things you can buy for your character that aren’t good but they give you more points to spend somewhere else. I always wanted to do something like that.
